title: High-Rate Free-Space Continuous-Variable QKD with Self-Referenced Passive State Preparation
authors: H. W. Yin, X. J. Liao, Y. H. Xu, P. Huang, K. T. Zhu, T. Wang, G. H. Zeng
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CVQKD) using passive state preparation (PSP) offers low-cost, high-rate secure communication. However, the existing PSP-CVQKD scheme with a transmitted local oscillator has high photon leakage noise and poor stability, making it unsuitable for high-loss transmission. In this work, for the first time, we propose and implement a local local oscillator (LLO) CVQKD system using a self-referenced (SR) PSP scheme, and give a theoretical proof of the equivalence of the PSP and GMCS protocol using temporal-mode theory. By employing the novel self-referenced pilot scheme to achieve high-precision time-varying frequency and phase compensation algorithms, we significantly improve the system' s signal-to-noise ratio and stability. The system achieves a record-high asymptotic secret key rate of 10.34 Mbps over a free-space channel with up to 23.5 dB loss, while maintaining low excess noise and robust performance under turbulent conditions. This work establishes the feasibility of SR-LLO CVQKD, providing a practical pathway toward secure, high-rate quantum communication in realistic environments.
summary: This paper demonstrates a high-rate CVQKD system that uses a local local oscillator and passive state preparation to overcome the noise and stability issues of transmitted local oscillators. By using a self-referenced pilot scheme, the authors achieved a high-speed secret key rate of over 10 Mbps even through significant atmospheric turbulence. This provides a practical pathway for robust, high-speed quantum communication in realistic free-space environments.
main_problem: Existing Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution (CVQKD) schemes using transmitted local oscillators suffer from high photon leakage noise and instability, making them unsuitable for high-loss free-space channels.
main_result: The authors implemented a Local Local Oscillator (LLO) CVQKD system with self-referenced passive state preparation, achieving a record-high asymptotic secret key rate of 10.34 Mbps over a 23.5 dB loss free-space channel.
method: The researchers used a thermal light source (ASE) for passive state preparation and a self-referenced pilot scheme (beacon light) to implement high-precision frequency and phase compensation algorithms via digital signal processing.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of a thermal source (ASE) at Alice's side, a turbulent free-space channel, and a receiver (Bob) using a separate local laser. The system utilizes an Integrated Coherent Receiver (ICR) with 20 GHz bandwidth for heterodyne detection.
key_observables: Secret key rate (SKR), excess noise (including PSP noise, frequency offset, and phase drift), channel transmittance, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
important_parameters: Repetition rate of 20 GHz, channel loss up to 23.5 dB, detection bandwidth of 20 GHz, and a thermal source with a second-order correlation function near 2.
main_assumptions: The demonstration is a proof-of-principle; the experiment used simplified coupling efficiency losses rather than full atmospheric complexity, and data processing was performed offline.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the system schematic; Figure 2 details the experimental setup for Alice and Bob; Figure 3 shows the optical layout; Figure 5 displays complex power spectra before and after frequency correction; Figure 6 and 7 show transmittance, noise components, and key rate performance under turbulence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of CVQKD in free space, proposes a new LLO-PSP scheme, provides a theoretical proof of equivalence to GMCS protocols, describes the experimental setup and DSP algorithms, analyzes noise and key rates, and concludes with future outlooks.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems due to its use of thermal state fluctuations for state preparation, the application of temporal-mode theory to continuous-time modes, and the management of decoherence-like effects (phase/frequency drift) in a turbulent channel.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
